results.title_inferred: The Seven Prakriti (Constitutional Types) and Differential Drug Response
results.original_text: Sharir Sthana — Human constitution and pharmacogenomics precursor. This chapter in the body science section documents the seven Prakriti (constitutions) based on the three doshas and their combinations. Most significant for modern medicine: documentation that different constitution types respond differently to the same medicines. Vata-dominant individuals: respond well to warm, oily, sweet medicines; respond badly to dry, cold, bitter ones. Pitta-dominant: respond well to cool, sweet, bitter; poorly to hot, pungent. Kapha-dominant: respond well to pungent, bitter, astringent; poorly to sweet, cold, heavy. This Charaka pharmacogenomic principle — that the same medicine has different effects on different individuals based on constitutional type — is now being re-examined through the lens of pharmacogenomics and metabolomics. The dosha constitutions may correspond to metabolic phenotypes with documented differential responses to specific drugs and foods.
results.translation: Sharirasthana [Body Science Section] — On Human Constitution as a Precursor to Pharmacogenomics. This chapter of the section treating the sciences of the body sets forth the seven Prakriti [constitutional types], derived from the three doshas [fundamental bio-humors] and their various combinations. Of greatest significance for contemporary medicine is the systematic documentation that different constitutional types respond differently to the same medicinal substances. Individuals of Vata-dominant constitution respond favorably to medicines that are warm [ushna], unctuous [snigdha], and sweet [madhura] in quality, and respond adversely to those that are dry [ruksha], cold [shita], and bitter [tikta]. Those of Pitta-dominant constitution respond favorably to medicines that are cool [shita], sweet [madhura], and bitter [tikta], and respond adversely to those that are hot [ushna] and pungent [katu]. Those of Kapha-dominant constitution respond favorably to medicines that are pungent [katu], bitter [tikta], and astringent [kashaya] in quality, and respond adversely to those that are sweet [madhura], cold [shita], and heavy [guru]. This foundational Charaka principle — that one and the same medicine produces differing effects upon different individuals according to their constitutional type — is now being revisited through the investigative frameworks of pharmacogenomics and metabolomics. It is proposed that the dosha-based constitutions may correspond to distinct metabolic phenotypes exhibiting documented differential responses to specific drugs and foodstuffs.

entities_mentioned.name: Prakriti
entities_mentioned.notes: The seven Prakriti types are derived from the three doshas singly and in combination (Vata, Pitta, Kapha, Vata-Pitta, Vata-Kapha, Pitta-Kapha, and Sama/balanced). Contemporary research explores correlations with genetic, metabolomic, and microbiome profiles.

entities_mentioned.name: Vata
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the three fundamental doshas; governs movement, dryness, and nervous system functions. Favorable medicines are warm, oily, and sweet; unfavorable are dry, cold, and bitter.

entities_mentioned.name: Pitta
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the three fundamental doshas; governs transformation, heat, and digestion. Favorable medicines are cool, sweet, and bitter; unfavorable are hot and pungent.

entities_mentioned.name: Kapha
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the three fundamental doshas; governs structure, cohesion, and lubrication. Favorable medicines are pungent, bitter, and astringent; unfavorable are sweet, cold, and heavy.

entities_mentioned.name: Ushna (warm/hot quality)
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the twenty gunas [qualities] used in Ayurvedic pharmacology to classify drug action.

entities_mentioned.name: Snigdha (unctuous/oily quality)
entities_mentioned.notes: Refers to the oily, smooth quality of a substance; promotes lubrication and nourishment in Ayurvedic pharmacology.

entities_mentioned.name: Rasas (tastes: madhura, tikta, katu, kashaya, etc.)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Phytochemical class proxies (e.g., bitter alkaloids, pungent terpenoids, astringent tannins)
entities_mentioned.notes: The six tastes [shadrasa] in Ayurveda — sweet (madhura), sour (amla), salty (lavana), pungent (katu), bitter (tikta), astringent (kashaya) — are used to predict pharmacological action and constitutional compatibility.
